Reverse-engineer the ARCHITECTURE of: **bodhi (54.07, rank 3)**

THE LEADERBOARD ON OUR EVALUATOR (GPT-5.5) — this is the ONLY board that matters to us:
  cellcog-max 55.78 | WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78 | bodhi 54.07 | lunon 53.51 | dalpha-deepresearch 53.10
  sourcery 51.17 | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 (== the "reference", so parity = 0.50) | openai-deepresearch 47.84
  POLARIS (us) 43.82 | perplexity-Research 43.05 | grok-deeper-search 41.22
NOTE: the widely-cited "ADORE 52.65" is on NEITHER board — treat it as unverified. The legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro
board (qianfan 58.03, ZTE 57.27, ...) is a DIFFERENT evaluator and its numbers are NOT comparable to ours.
We already hold 100-task artifacts for: cellcog, tavily, onyx, deepinsight, baidu-qianfan (in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus).

DO BOTH of these:
1. THE PUBLISHED RECORD. Search hard: arXiv, GitHub, company blog, HF model/dataset card, tech report, WeChat/Zhihu
   posts (several of these are Chinese teams — search Chinese-language sources too: 深度研究, 智能体, 报告生成).
   Is there a paper? Code? A tech report? If there is genuinely NOTHING public, SAY SO — do not invent an architecture.
2. THE ARTIFACT. If we have its reports in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus (or the Acquire phase just fetched them), READ THEM and measure
   them across MANY tasks, not just task 72. Reverse-engineer the generation process from the text: section/subsection
   structure, paragraph length distribution, whether it uses bullets/tables (and whether that varies BY TASK GENRE),
   how it cites, whether it names studies in prose, how it handles conflicting evidence, whether it has a dedicated
   synthesis/tensions section, whether the writing looks single-pass or multi-step, whether there is evidence of a
   revision/critique pass.

THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS MOST: **how does it COMPOSE the report, and how does it produce SYNTHESIS?**
We are an extraction-grounded fact conveyor — we span-verify every sentence, which forbids cross-source inference.
We score insight 0.4238 (weight 0.32, our worst). Find out what these systems do INSTEAD.

Prefer primary sources. Mark anything unverified. We were burned tonight by a confidently-quoted stale number.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review

import json,re,statistics as st
def load(f):
    return {json.loads(l)['id']: json.loads(l)['article'] for l in open(f)}
files=['bodhi.jsonl','cellcog-max.jsonl','WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612.jsonl','gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl','openai-deepresearch.jsonl']
for f in files:
    d=load(f)
    W=[];H2=[];H3=[];TBL=[];BUL=[];CIT=[];PARA=[];URLS=[]
    for a in d.values():
        w=len(re.findall(r'\w+',a)) # rough
        # CJK aware
        cjk=len(re.findall(r'[一-鿿]',a))
        W.append(w+cjk)
        H2.append(len(re.findall(r'^##\s',a,re.M)))
        H3.append(len(re.findall(r'^###\s',a,re.M)))
        TBL.append(len(re.findall(r'^\|',a,re.M)))
        BUL.append(len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*+]\s',a,re.M)))
        CIT.append(len(re.findall(r'\[\d+\]|\[\[\d+\]|\(https?://',a)))
        URLS.append(len(re.findall(r'https?://',a)))
        ps=[p for p in a.split(chr(10)+chr(10)) if len(p)>50 and not p.strip().startswith(('#','|','-','*'))]
        if ps: PARA.append(st.mean(len(p) for p in ps))
    m=lambda x: round(st.mean(x),1)
    print(f'{f:42s} words={m(W):7} h2={m(H2):5} h3={m(H3):5} tblrows={m(TBL):6} bullets={m(BUL):6} cites={m(CIT):6} urls={m(URLS):6} avg_para_chars={m(PARA)}')
"

content.content:
PROMPT: Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, Engl
LEN 41791
## Scope, assumptions, and how “AI-driven restructuring” is treated in this review
- **Scope**: peer‑reviewed, English‑language **journal articles** on how AI (including machine learning and, where covered, generative AI) is restructuring labor markets as part of the **Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR)**—i.e., the broader integration of AI with digital platforms, data infrastructures, cyber‑physical systems, and new organizational forms.
- **Definition of “restructuring”** used here: persistent changes in **(i)** task composition within jobs, **(ii)** occupational and skill mix within firms/industries, **(iii)** hiring and training strategies, **(iv)** wage structures and bargaining power, and **(v)** market structure and geographic concentration.
- **Evidence constraint**: I only cite journal articles. Where the research record contains only bibliographic indications of a paper’s existence (without extractable results), I either do not use it or explicitly limit claims to what is visible on the journal landing page.

---

## 1) Core conceptual frameworks used to analyze AI-driven labor-market restructuring

### 1.1 Task-based view of AI: displacement, augmentation, reinstatement, and job redesign
A dominant framing treats occupations as **bundles of tasks**, with AI affecting subsets of tasks rather than “automating entire jobs.” Brynjolfsson, Mitchell, and Rock argue that ML (as a subfield of AI) changes which tasks are technically automatable by circumventing parts of **Polanyi’s paradox**, implying that the key impact is often **job redesign and business-process reengineering** rather than immediate occupation replacement [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). They emphasize that “most occupations” contain some ML-suitable tasks but few contain only such tasks, implying widespread **partial automation** and **task rebundling** [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).

Acemoglu and Restrepo’s task-based logic (in their “direction of AI” argument) emphasizes that automation always reduces labor’s share in the automated tasks, and net labor-demand effects depend on countervailing mechanisms such as productivity/scale effects and new task creation (reinstatement), with the warning that “so-so automation” may displace labor without large productivity gains [[113]](https://economics.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/The%20Wrong%20Kind%20of%20AI%20-%20Artificial%20Intelligence%20and.pdf). This framework is widely used to interpret heterogeneous outcomes (job loss vs job transformation) and motivates measurement strategies that map AI capabilities to tasks.

**Restructuring implication**: even when aggregate employment effects are small in the short run, firms may still be actively reorganizing work—shifting hiring away from some roles, rewriting skill requirements, and investing in complementary training and organizational changes.

---

### 1.2 AI as a General-Purpose Technology (GPT) with long lags and complementary co-invention
Brynjolfsson, Mitchell, and Rock explicitly frame ML as a **general-purpose technology** (pervasive, improving over time, and generating complementary innovation), emphasizing **diffusion lags** and the need for co-invention and organizational redesign to unlock value [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). They also highlight that realized macro effects may lag behind technical potential because adoption requires reconfiguring workflows, measurement, incentives, and coordination inside firms [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).

**Restructuring implication**: labor-market impacts may appear first as within-firm changes (tasks, job design, training, monitoring) and only later as sectoral reallocation, wage structure shifts, and measurable aggregate employment changes.

---

### 1.3 Strategic management and organizational complements: exposure, capability, and uneven adoption
Felten, Raj, and Seamans emphasize that empirical progress depends on better measures of **exposure to AI**, proposing occupational, industry, and geographic exposure constructs to enable systematic study across units (firms, industries, places) [[11]](https://collaborate.princeton.edu/en/publications/occupational-industry-and-geographic-exposure-to-artificial-intel/). A recurring theme in this strand is **complementarity**: AI’s value depends on data, talent, and organizational design.

Consistent with this, Han et al. show that AI investment (proxied by AI-skill demands in postings) can increase **firm resilience** to natural disaster shocks, but realized productivity benefits are constrained by a lack of complementary organizational designs [[94]](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/isre.2022.0440). This reinforces the idea that restructuring is partly about building complementary organizational capabilities (workflows, governance, integration) rather than simply “installing AI.”

---

### 1.4 Labor-process and organizational-control perspectives: algorithmic management as restructuring of power and supervision
Beyond production tasks, AI-enabled systems restructure **management, monitoring, and control**. Kellogg, Valentine, and Christin synthesize “algorithmic control” as a contested terrain, identifying mechanisms through which algorithms direct, evaluate, and discipline workers (“6 Rs”), and documenting worker resistance (“algoactivism”) and new algorithmic occupations [[117]](https://onwork.edu.au/bibitem/2020-Kellogg,Katherine+C-Valentine,Melissa+A-etal-Algorithms+at+Work+The+New+Contested+Terrain+of+Control/). De Stefano and Taes focus on algorithmic management as a qualitative transformation of work (surveillance, automated decision-making, privacy, discrimination) and argue for the regulatory role of collective bargaining [[116]](https://lirias.kuleuven.be/retrieve/722036).

## 1. 关系谱系：国内金融机构间“投资—借贷”主要层次与制度结构

### 1.1 无担保同业资金融通：同业拆借（Interbank Lending）
**法律与交易结构**：同业拆借被界定为经人民银行批准进入同业拆借市场的金融机构之间、通过全国统一同业拆借网络进行的**无担保资金融通**[[1]](https://www.pbc.gov.cn/zhengwugongkai/attachDir/2025/11/2025111914190098678.pdf)。交易以逐笔合同为基础，拆借利率由双方商定；清算通过大额支付系统/转账完成；不得现金清算[[1]](https://www.pbc.gov.cn/zhengwugongkai/attachDir/2025/11/2025111914190098678.pdf)。  
**关键风控约束**（对网络形态很重要）：  
- **准入与机构范围**明确覆盖银行与多类非银金融机构[[1]](https://www.pbc.gov.cn/zhengwugongkai/attachDir/2025/11/2025111914190098678.pdf)，决定网络节点集合。  
- **期限上限与不得展期**（且到期不得展期）直接限制滚续链条长度与期限错配累积[[1]](https://www.pbc.gov.cn/zhengwugongkai/attachDir/2025/11/2025111914190098678.pdf)。  
- **拆借限额**按机构类型与资本/存款基数核定，约束边权上限并使网络更“受控稀疏”[[1]](https://www.pbc.gov.cn/zhengwugongkai/attachDir/2025/11/2025111914190098678.pdf)。  

**系统性风险含义**：无担保同业拆借的传染主要通过**对手方信用风险**与**流动性挤兑/不续作**传播；当信心下降时，缩短期限、减少拆出、提高利率会迅速引发资金分层与“核心

        b=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'\s*([-*+•]|\d+[.)]\s)',l)]
        n=len(lines) or 1
        pr.append(len(p)/n); bu.append(len(b)/n)
        tb.append(len([l for l in lines if l.startswith('|')])/n)
        if p: plen.append(st.mean(len(l) for l in p))
        named.append(len(NAMED.findall(r['article'])))
    m=lambda x: round(st.mean(x),2)
    print(f'{f:35s} prose_lines={m(pr)} bullet={m(bu)} table={m(tb)} | mean_prose_line_chars={int(st.mean(plen))} | named-study-in-prose/rep={m(named)}')
"

input.architecture:
PUBLISHED RECORD: essentially nothing. No arXiv paper, no GitHub, no HF model/dataset card, no tech report, no Chinese-language writeup (I searched 深度研究/智能体/报告生成 -- zero hits; it is not a Chinese team). The ONLY public statements are marketing: the product page, a Threads post, and LinkedIn posts. The full technical content of the LinkedIn post is: "The reasoning engine behind Sapient Bodhi is now ranked #3 in the world... Bodhi Deep Research powers multiple enterprise agents across the platform." Product pages add only: multi-agent, an "Enterprise Context Graph" shared-context layer, shared memory, model-agnostic routing, runs on Amazon Bedrock + AgentCore. NOTE a live contradiction in their own materials: the product page claims "#1 spot on the Hugging Face DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard" while their own social posts say #3 -- treat all their marketing numbers as unreliable. Everything below is reverse-engineered from the 100 artifacts, not from any disclosure.

ARTIFACT-DERIVED ARCHITECTURE (n=100, gpt55_board/bodhi.jsonl):

(1) RETRIEVE-FIRST, PRE-NUMBERED GLOBAL SOURCE LIBRARY. Citations render as [[n]](url). In 99/99 reports each index n maps to exactly ONE url -- a stable global index. Mean max index = 128.7 (median 121, max 280), but only 61.3 unique URLs are actually cited: ~50% of numbered slots are NEVER used. In 47/99 reports the first citation in the document is not [1]. So numbering is NOT order-of-first-appearance -- it is assigned by a research phase that runs BEFORE composition, which builds a ~130-280-item numbered library, of which the writer selects about half. Over-retrieve, then curate.

(2) THE COMPOSER HOLDS THE WHOLE LIBRARY (not section-scoped retrieval). Decisive test: correlation between position-in-document and citation index. bodhi = +0.279, and the first 20% of the document already draws on 83% of the report's full index range. Compare WhaleCloud-DocChain = +0.872 with only 27% of the range used in the first 20% -- that is the signature of section-by-section composition with sources appended as they are encountered. bodhi's near-flat correlation means the writer had the entire source pool addressable from sentence one. (sourcery is even flatter, +0.018/96%.)

(3) SCOPE CONTRACT AS SECTION ZERO. Reports open not with an executive summary but with a definitional/measurement-convention contract: it states its own assumptions, date cutoff, definition of the key term, and its evidence policy. Verbatim examples: "Scope, assumptions, and how 'AI-driven restructuring' is treated in this review... Evidence constraint: I only cite journal articles"; "以下报告在...做两点假设并据此展开"; "我会在对应条目中明确'公司自述/媒体转述/监管披露'的证据强度" (I will explicitly mark evidence strength as company-self-report vs media vs regulatory-filing). First H2 is very often a 口径 (measurement-convention) section, e.g. "1) 数据口径与'为什么你可能看到不同的历史高点'". This is almost certainly what drives instruction_following 54.41.

(4) BESPOKE OUTLINE, ARGUMENTATIVE HEADINGS. 1186 H2s across 100 reports; the most-repeated H2 appears only 4 times. There is NO boilerplate outline. Headings carry claims, not topics: "不是'谁热'，而是谁能穿越周期" (not who's hot, but who survives the cycle), "谁真有空间，谁只是表面热闹" (who has real room and who is just superficially hot). ~11.9 H2 / 29.1 H3 per report.

(5) GENRE-ADAPTIVE FORMATTING. 66/100 reports contain ZERO table rows. Tables appear only when the task demands a comparison matrix (横向比较, "evaluate their input signals and accuracy", hardware-PM comparison). Contrast WhaleCloud (135 table rows/report avg) and cellcog (87) which table everything. Length variance is huge: 9k to 147k chars, stdev/mean = 0.56. bodhi sizes and formats to the task, not to a target.

(6) LENGTH IS NOT THE LEVER. bodhi is the SHORTEST of the top group (~10.7k words vs cellcog 23.9k, gemini 16.9k) and still beats gemini by 4 points. Within bodhi's own 100, corr(length, insight) = +0.05 and corr(length, readability) = -0.21. Insight-per-word, not word count.

(7) OUTPUT IS A BRIEF, NOT AN ESSAY. 53% of lines are bullets, only 25% prose lines, mean prose line 260 chars. It does NOT rely on named-study-in-prose (0.78/report, the LOWEST of the pack -- cellcog is 2.96). The Brynjolfsson/Acemoglu prose in task 72 is an exception the literature-review task forced.

(8) NO REVISION/CRITIC PASS IS VISIBLE, and no reference list: 92/100 have no bibliography section (citations are inline hyperlinks only). Consistency of index-to-URL mapping and the tight score band (insight 0.483-0.635) suggest a disciplined single composition pass over a curated library, rather than a critique-and-rewrite loop. UNVERIFIED -- absence of evidence.
input.composition_method:
THE ANSWER: bodhi writes in TWO EXPLICITLY DEMARCATED REGISTERS, and the synthesis lives entirely in the second one.

Register A -- EVIDENCE. Densely cited: 4.97 citations per 1000 chars, and 72% of substantive sentences carry at least one [[n]](url). Each of ~61 cited sources is re-cited ~4.06 times. THIS IS THE CRITICAL POINT FOR US: bodhi is NOT less grounded than POLARIS. It grounds at roughly the same rate we do. Grounding density is not the difference.

Register B -- INFERENCE, marked by a bolded label. I extracted every line of the form `**Label**: text`. Lines whose label is an inference word (implication / 含义 / 机制 / 解读 / 判断 / why / takeaway / interpretation) are UNCITED 67% of the time (137 uncited vs 69 cited). Lines with any other label -- the evidence register -- are uncited only 32% of the time. That 2x gap is the whole architecture. The bold label LICENSES an uncited, cross-source inferential sentence.

Real examples of Register B (all uncited):
- "**Restructuring implication**: even when aggregate employment effects are small in the short run, firms may still be actively reorganizing work -- shifting hiring away from some roles, rewriting skill requirements..."
- "**统一解读** (unified interpretation): the metal is still Au and the work function did not change, yet merely reducing contamination/damage (UHV) pushed Rc down -- this directly proves that work-function matching is NOT the first-order determinant; interface states / residue / damage-induced pinning are."
- "**机制差异**: Europe/US are more mature in open licensing, APIs and open-data ecosystems; China is stronger on national platformization and infrastructure-ization, but reusable learning objects + open licensing + data interoperability remain the next-stage bottleneck."
That second one is precisely the move POLARIS forbids: it reads several papers and states what they JOINTLY prove. No single span supports it.

THE LABELS ARE PER-REPORT, NOT A GLOBAL TEMPLATE. 1720 DISTINCT bolded labels across 100 reports (1955 uses). Within one report a label recurs (统一解读 x11, 传导机制 x10, 为什么它强 x13, Restructuring implication x5) but across reports they are almost all unique. So bodhi INVENTS A TASK-SPECIFIC ANALYTIC RUBRIC per report -- a small set of named analytic slots -- then applies it to every evidence unit. That is what makes the synthesis feel systematic rather than decorative: the same 2-4 questions get asked of every piece of evidence.

HOW IT HANDLES CONFLICTING EVIDENCE -- COUNTERINTUITIVE. bodhi has the FEWEST conflict markers of the top systems (4.5/report vs cellcog 12.4, gemini 8.3, WhaleCloud 8.6) and only 3/100 have a "limitations" heading. It does NOT have a tensions section. Instead it dissolves conflict ARCHITECTURALLY: the opening 口径/definitions section explains WHY sources disagree (different measurement conventions), so the entire report is organized as a reconciliation. It also grades sources by type in-line (company-self-report vs media vs regulatory filing) rather than flagging disagreement.

WHERE SYNTHESIS SITS. Two places, both non-standard. (a) Distributed: an inference line after nearly every evidence unit -- synthesis is continuous, not a terminal chapter. (b) Terminal sections are USER-DIRECTED and second-person, re-binding everything to the reader's decision: "你当前问题的'最关键结论'（浓缩版）" (the most critical conclusion FOR YOUR question, condensed), "如果你是找职业方向，分别适合谁？", "你可直接拿来用的'公司清单'", "本回答的证据缺口" (the evidence gaps in THIS answer). 38/96 end on a synthesis-type heading; median position of the first synthesis heading is 0.84 through the doc. Several reports even close by OFFERING follow-on deliverables to the user. bodhi treats the report as an answer to a person, not as a document.

Some reports also front-load a verdict ("先给结论" / "Bottom line" / "Executive Summary" as H2), but only ~13/100 -- it is not the dominant pattern.

input.performance:
bodhi 54.07 = rank 3. Per-dimension: Comprehensiveness 54.15, Insight 54.60, Instruction Following 54.41, Readability 51.87.

Its profile vs the pack:
- INSIGHT is its best dimension (54.60) -- 2nd highest on the whole board behind cellcog-max (57.08), ahead of WhaleCloud (55.33) and lunon (54.83). Against POLARIS's 42.38, that is a ~12-point gap on the 0.32-weight dimension.
- READABILITY is its WORST (51.87) and it does not care. The price of ~250 inline [[n]](url) hyperlinks per report. Note dalpha ranks 5th overall with the board's BEST readability (53.20) -- readability is not how you climb here.
- It achieves this with the SHORTEST reports in the top tier (~10.7k words vs cellcog's 23.9k). It is the highest insight-per-word system on the board.
- It is unusually FLAT: per-task insight ranges only 0.483-0.635. No blowups, no failures (100/100 scored). This consistency is itself an architectural signal -- the bespoke-rubric-per-report method transfers across all 22 fields.
- Within bodhi's own 100 tasks, the features that correlate (weakly, all |r|<0.3) with insight are: tables +0.21, uncited-inference-lines +0.18, unique sources +0.16. Length correlates with NOTHING (+0.05) and hurts readability (-0.21).
what_we_can_steal: THE BIG ONE -- ADOPT A TWO-REGISTER PROSE POLICY INSTEAD OF LIFTING SPAN-VERIFICATION. Do not weaken grounding: bodhi cites 72% of its substantive sentences, roughly what we do. Instead, ADD a second, syntactically-marked register. Keep span-verification mandatory for every sentence in the evidence register. Then permit a bolded, labelled INFERENCE line (`**Implication**:`, `**Mechanism**:`, `**Net verdict**:`) that is EXEMPT from span-verification and is REQUIRED to reference >=2 already-cited evidence units from the section. The bold label is what makes it safe and legible -- it tells reader and judge 'this is analysis, not a sourced fact'. This gives us cross-source inference without abandoning the fact-conveyor guarantee. Measured: 67% of bodhi's inference lines are uncited vs 32% of its evidence lines.

PER-REPORT BESPOKE ANALYTIC RUBRIC. After planning, have the model invent 2-4 NAMED analytic slots for THIS task (bodhi: 1720 distinct labels over 100 reports -- e.g. 'Restructuring implication', '统一解读', '传导机制', '为什么它强', '关键风险'). Then apply the SAME slots to every evidence unit in the report. Systematic repetition of a task-appropriate question is what makes the synthesis read as analysis rather than as decoration. This is cheap: it is one extra planning call producing a 4-item list.

GIVE THE COMPOSER THE WHOLE SOURCE LIBRARY, NOT SECTION-SCOPED SNIPPETS. Cross-source inference is IMPOSSIBLE if the writer only sees one section's sources. bodhi's citation-index-vs-position correlation is +0.279 (vs WhaleCloud's +0.872) -- it draws from the full pool starting in section 1. If we retrieve per-section, we have architecturally forbidden the very insight we are trying to buy.

OVER-RETRIEVE THEN CURATE. Build a numbered library of ~130-280 sources and let the writer cite only ~60 of them (bodhi leaves ~50% of numbered slots unused). Never-cited sources still shape the writer's judgement of what is consensus vs outlier. Do not prune the library to what will be cited.

SECTION ZERO = SCOPE CONTRACT, NOT EXECUTIVE SUMMARY. Open by declaring: our definition of the key term, the date cutoff, the evidence-inclusion rule ('only peer-reviewed journal articles'), and an evidence-strength taxonomy ('company self-report vs media vs regulatory filing'). This is likely a large part of bodhi's 54.41 instruction_following, and it is nearly free to add.

RESOLVE CONFLICT ARCHITECTURALLY, NOT WITH A TENSIONS SECTION. bodhi has the FEWEST conflict markers of the top systems (4.5/report) and almost never has a limitations section. It instead makes measurement-convention (口径) the FIRST section -- explaining WHY sources disagree -- so the whole report reads as a reconciliation. For any task where sources give different numbers, lead with 'here is why the numbers differ' rather than listing both and shrugging.

MAKE HEADINGS ARGUMENTATIVE. bodhi's 1186 H2s have a max repeat of 4 -- zero boilerplate outline. Headings assert claims ('not who's hot, but who survives the cycle'; 'who has real room and who is just superficially hot'). A claim-bearing heading is free insight surface that costs no tokens of body text. Kill any fixed section template we have.

STOP BUYING LENGTH. bodhi is rank 3 with the SHORTEST reports in the top tier (10.7k words, less than half of cellcog). Within its own 100 tasks, length correlates +0.05 with insight and -0.21 with readability. Our recent flywheel work chasing 8055 words is pushing on a rope. Spend the budget on inference lines, not on more sections.

GENRE-GATE THE TABLES. 66/100 bodhi reports have ZERO tables; tables appear ONLY for explicitly comparative tasks. Tabling everything (WhaleCloud, cellcog) is not what separates rank 3 from rank 7. But within bodhi, tables are its single strongest per-task correlate of insight (+0.21) -- so emit them when, and only when, the task is a comparison.

CLOSE IN SECOND PERSON, BOUND TO THE USER'S DECISION. bodhi's final sections are 'the most critical conclusion FOR YOUR question (condensed)', 'if you are choosing a career direction, who suits whom?', 'a company list you can use directly', 'the evidence gaps in THIS answer'. It answers a person, not a topic. Cheap to add; likely helps both insight and instruction_following.
unknowns: The model(s) behind Bodhi Deep Research. Publicis Sapient says only 'model-agnostic' and 'powered by Amazon Bedrock and AgentCore'. Bedrock implies Claude/Nova are available to it, but NOTHING confirms which model wrote these reports.

Whether the plan/outline is generated before or after retrieval. The stable global citation index proves retrieval precedes composition, but not whether the OUTLINE precedes retrieval. Both are consistent with the artifacts.

Whether there is a revision/critique pass. I found no textual evidence of one (no residue, no reference list, tight score band), but absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. UNVERIFIED either way.

Whether composition is literally one LLM call or a section-parallel writer with shared global source context. The citation-spread test rules out section-SCOPED RETRIEVAL, but not section-parallel WRITING against a shared library.
The number of search/tool calls, the retrieval depth, and any reranking. We only see ~130-280 numbered survivors, not the funnel above them.

Whether the bespoke bold labels are model-invented per report or drawn from a large internal library of analytic frames. 1720 distinct labels over 100 reports is consistent with either, though the tight fit to each task's domain favours per-report invention.

The Publicis Sapient product page's '#1 on DeepResearch Bench' claim directly contradicts their own '#3' social posts. Which (if either) refers to a real leaderboard state is unresolved -- do NOT quote either number.
Nothing about the 'Enterprise Context Graph' is verifiable from these reports; it is likely an enterprise-deployment feature not exercised by the benchmark.
